preamble: The transformation of voiceless bilabial stop [p] to the voiceless alveolar affricate [ts] is a common case of labial palatalization, where the place of articulation shifts significantly forward.

examples.note: Bantu languages frequently show labial consonants becoming dental or alveolar affricates under the influence of the palatal glide [j] or front vowels.
While initially becoming the labiodental affricate [pf], further shifts in articulation can lead to dental/alveolar affricates.

sources: Ladefoged, P., & Maddieson, I. (1996). The Sounds of the World's Languages.
Schenker, A. M. (1995). The Dawn of Slavic.
